children.title: Structure & Function of the External Eye and Cornea
Eyelids
Layers (anterior → posterior): skin → orbicularis oculi → orbital septum → pre-tarsal fat → tarsus → conjunctiva
Tarsus: dense fibrous plate; contains Meibomian glands (sebaceous; lipid layer of tear film)
Upper lid retractors: levator palpebrae (CN III) + Müller muscle (sympathetic)
Lower lid retractors: capsulopalpebral fascia + inferior tarsal muscle (sympathetic)
Orbicularis oculi: CN VII; pretarsal (blink) + preseptal + orbital parts
Glands of Zeis (sebaceous) and Moll (apocrine sweat): at lash line
Lymphatics: medial (submandibular nodes) + lateral (preauricular nodes)
Lacrimal Functional Unit
Lacrimal gland: orbital + palpebral lobes; CN VII (greater superficial petrosal) secretomotor; accessory glands: Krause + Wolfring
Tear drainage: puncta → canaliculi → common canaliculus → lacrimal sac → nasolacrimal duct → inferior meatus
Valve of Hasner: mucosal fold at NLD opening; imperforate = congenital NLD obstruction
Tear Film
Modern model: mucoaqueous layer (mucin gradient, thicker at epithelium) + lipid layer (top)
Lipid layer: Meibomian glands; prevents evaporation; ~0.1μm thick
Aqueous layer: lacrimal gland (main + accessory); electrolytes, proteins, IgA, lysozyme, lactoferrin
Mucin: goblet cells (MUC5AC) + surface epithelium (MUC1, MUC4, MUC16); wettability
Tear break-up time (TBUT): normal >10 sec; <5 sec = abnormal
Functions: lubrication, antimicrobial, oxygen supply to avascular cornea, smooth optical surface
Conjunctiva
Regions: palpebral (lines lids) + fornix + bulbar (covers globe to limbus)
Epithelium: stratified non-keratinized squamous; goblet cells highest density in inferior nasal fornix
Substantia propria: loose connective tissue; contains lymphocytes, mast cells, plasma cells
Blood supply: anterior ciliary arteries + palpebral branches; lymphatics drain to preauricular + submandibular nodes
Conjunctival-associated lymphoid tissue (CALT): local immune defense
Sclera
Dense irregular collagen (type I); avascular; blue tint in infants (thin), yellow with age (lipid deposition)
Three layers: episclera (vascular) → stroma → lamina fusca (inner)
Emissary canals: vortex veins, ciliary nerves/vessels pass through
Lamina cribrosa: specialized scleral region where optic nerve exits; vulnerable in glaucoma
Cornea
Zones
Central optical zone: ~3-4mm; most regular; most refractive power (~43D)
Paracentral: 4-7mm; flattens peripherally
Peripheral: 7-11mm; junction with limbus
5 Layers
Epithelium: 5-7 cell layers; rapidly regenerating (~7 days); tight junctions (barrier); stem cells at limbus
Bowman layer: acellular condensed collagen; does NOT regenerate; breach = scar
Stroma: 90% of corneal thickness; collagen type I in regular orthogonal lamellae → transparency; keratocytes; proteoglycans (keratan sulfate, dermatan sulfate)
Descemet membrane: basement membrane of endothelium; thickens with age (3μm at birth → 10-12μm adult); elastic; resists perforation
Endothelium: single monolayer; Na/K-ATPase pump keeps stroma dehydrated; does NOT regenerate; normal density ~2500 cells/mm²; loss leads to corneal oedema
Properties: avascular, transparent, ~0.52mm central / ~0.67mm peripheral thickness
Innervation: densely innervated by CN V1 (nasociliary → long ciliary nerves); most sensitive tissue in body
Limbus
Corneoscleral junction; site of limbal stem cells (LSC) for corneal epithelial regeneration
Palisades of Vogt: radial stromal ridges housing LSC niche; most prominent superiorly and inferiorly
Site of surgical incisions (cataract, trabeculectomy)
Defense Mechanisms
Physical: blink reflex, eyelid closure, tear drainage, epithelial barrier
Chemical: lysozyme, lactoferrin, IgA, defensins, lacritin in tears
Immune: CALT, conjunctival lymphocytes + mast cells; cornea is immune-privileged (avascularity, low MHC expression)
Examination Techniques
External Eye Exam
Inspection: symmetry, proptosis, lid position, ptosis, lagophthalmos, discharge, injection pattern
Corneal sensation: cotton wisp or Cochet-Bonnet aesthesiometer; reduced in HSV keratitis, neurotrophic keratopathy, contact lens wear, post-LASIK
Eversion of upper lid: essential to examine tarsal conjunctiva (giant papillae in VKC/GPC)
Slit-Lamp Biomicroscopy
Diffuse illumination: overview; general conjunctival and corneal assessment
Direct focal illumination: most used; detailed focal exam at any depth
Retro-illumination: backlit from iris/fundus; identifies corneal opacities, crystalline deposits, vacuoles
Specular reflection: endothelial cell mosaic; guttae (Fuchs)
Sclerotic scatter: total internal reflection; highlights corneal opacities (e.g., KC scarring)
Indirect illumination: adjacent to beam; subtle infiltrates, nerves
Ocular Surface Staining
Fluorescein: stains disrupted epithelium (intercellular gaps, erosions, ulcers); TBUT; Seidel test (aqueous leak)
Rose bengal: stains devitalized cells + mucus; very sensitive; stings; replaced by lissamine green
Lissamine green: similar to rose bengal; stains dead/devitalized cells; better tolerated
Tear Function Tests
Schirmer test I (no anesthesia): basic + reflex secretion; <10mm/5min = reduced (borderline); <5mm/5min = abnormal
Schirmer test II (with anesthesia): basic secretion only; <5mm/5min abnormal
Phenol red thread test: 15 sec; <9mm = reduced
Tear meniscus height: OCT measurement; <0.2mm = reduced
MMP-9 (InflammaDry): rapid point-of-care; elevated in inflammatory dry eye
Corneal Contour & Topography
Keratometry: measures central 3mm; K readings (K1 + K2); used for IOL calculations
Corneal topography (Placido disc): anterior surface curvature map; detects irregular astigmatism, keratoconus (inferior steepening, skewed axes)
Corneal tomography (Scheimpflug, e.g., Pentacam): anterior + posterior surface + pachymetry map; essential for refractive screening (detects forme fruste KC)
Keratoconus indices: KISA%, Amsler-Krumeich grading, Belin-Ambrosio deviation
Endothelial Assessment
Specular microscopy: cell density (normal ~2500/mm²), morphology (polymegethism = size variation, pleomorphism = shape variation)
Pachymetry: central corneal thickness; normal ~520-540μm; increased in endothelial dysfunction/oedema
Confocal microscopy (in vivo): all corneal layers; identifies Acanthamoeba cysts, fungal hyphae, nerve fiber density/tortuosity, keratocytes
Anterior segment OCT (AS-OCT): cross-sectional; angle assessment, corneal layers, pachymetry, epithelial mapping
UBM (ultrasound biomicroscopy): anterior segment structures; angle, ciliary body; useful when media opaque
Clinical Approach to Ocular Surface Disease
Conjunctival Signs of Inflammation
Hyperemia: superficial (conjunctival vessels; moveable with cotton tip) vs ciliary flush (deep, limbal, fixed; suggests anterior uveitis or keratitis)
Discharge: watery (viral/allergic), mucopurulent (bacterial), ropy/stringy (allergic/VKC)
Papillae: central vascular core + inflammatory cells; non-specific; giant papillae (>1mm): VKC, GPC (contact lens)
Follicles: lymphoid aggregates without central vessel; viral/chlamydial/toxic conjunctivitis
Pseudomembranes: fibrin on epithelium (removable); membranes: adherent (bleed on removal); severe inflammation (EKC, SJS, diphtheria)
Chemosis: conjunctival oedema; allergy, thyroid, venous obstruction
Subconjunctival haemorrhage: blood under conjunctiva; trauma, Valsalva, hypertension, bleeding diathesis
Symblepharon: adhesion of palpebral to bulbar conjunctiva; SJS, MMP, chemical burn, trachoma
Corneal Signs of Inflammation
Superficial punctate keratopathy (SPK): tiny epithelial erosions; pattern localizes cause (inferior: dry eye/exposure; superior: SLK/trachoma; diffuse: toxic/viral; interpalpebral: UV/dry eye)
Epithelial defect: fluorescein positive; ulcer if stromal involvement
Infiltrate: grey-white opacity; active cellular infiltration; marginal vs central; sterile vs infectious
Ulcer: epithelial + stromal loss; may have hypopyon if severe
Pannus: superficial vascularization; trachoma (superior), contact lens wear, limbal stem cell deficiency
Keratic precipitates (KP): on endothelium; fine (non-granulomatous uveitis) vs mutton-fat (granulomatous)
Corneal oedema: epithelial (microcysts, bullae) + stromal (Descemet folds, haziness)
Dry Eye Disease
Definition & Classification
DEWS II: multifactorial disease of ocular surface characterized by loss of homeostasis of tear film
Aqueous-deficient dry eye (ADDE): lacrimal gland failure (Sjögren, non-Sjögren)
Evaporative dry eye (EDE): most common; Meibomian gland dysfunction (MGD), eyelid disorders, low blink rate
Often mixed mechanism
Aqueous Tear Deficiency
Sjögren syndrome: primary (dry eye + dry mouth) or secondary (RA, SLE, SS); ANA, anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La; lip biopsy (focal lymphocytic sialadenitis)
Non-Sjögren: age-related lacrimal gland atrophy, lacrimal gland scarring (SJS, MMP, trachoma, radiation), reflex hyposecretion (CN V damage)
Evaporative Dry Eye (MGD)
Most common cause of dry eye; Meibomian gland atrophy, dropout, or obstruction → poor lipid layer
Signs: lid margin telangiectasia, gland inspissation, meibum quality (liquid → toothpaste-like), gland dropout on meibography
Associated with rosacea, seborrheic blepharitis, contact lens wear
Treatment (stepwise)
Step 1: artificial tears (preserved vs preservative-free), environmental modifications, lid hygiene, omega-3 supplements
Step 2: unpreserved tears, cyclosporine A 0.05%/0.1% (Restasis/Cequa), lifitegrast 5% (Xiidra), punctal plugs (silicone > collagen), warm compress + lid massage
Step 3: serum tears, scleral lenses, intense pulsed light (IPL) for MGD, oral doxycycline (MGD/rosacea)
Step 4: amniotic membrane, tarsorrhaphy, salivary gland transplantation
Emerging: Reproxalap (TRPM8), OC-01 (varenicline nasal spray = Tyrvaya), perfluorohexyloctane (Miebo)
Eyelid Conditions Associated With Ocular Surface Disease
Rosacea: chronic inflammatory skin disease; facial flushing, telangiectasia, acne; ocular = MGD + blepharitis + chalazia + SPK; treat with lid hygiene, doxycycline, IPL
Seborrheic blepharitis: greasy scales at lash bases; associated with scalp seborrhoea; treat with lid scrubs, baby shampoo, dandruff shampoo
Staphylococcal blepharitis: collarettes (fibrin cuffs) at lash bases; chronic infection + hypersensitivity; treat with lid hygiene + topical antibiotics
Marginal keratitis: peripheral sterile infiltrate 2° to staph hypersensitivity; separated from limbus by clear zone; treat with topical steroid ± antibiotic
Phlyctenular keratoconjunctivitis: nodular immune reaction (staph, TB antigen); limbal/corneal; treat topical steroids
Hordeolum (stye): acute Staphylococcal infection; internal (Meibomian gland) vs external (Zeis/Moll); warm compress ± incision
Chalazion: chronic lipogranulomatous inflammation of Meibomian gland; firm non-tender nodule; treat with warm compress → steroid injection → I&C
Eyelid, Conjunctival & Corneal Surface Disorders
Eyelid Disorders
Floppy eyelid syndrome: rubbery, easily everted upper lid; obese middle-aged men; associated with OSA; papillary conjunctivitis; treat with lid hygiene, CPAP, lid taping at night
Trichiasis: misdirected lashes touching globe; epilation, electrolysis, cryotherapy, laser, lid surgery
Distichiasis: accessory row of lashes from Meibomian gland orifices; congenital (lymphoedema-distichiasis, FOXC2); treat with cryotherapy or lid split
Conjunctival Disorders
Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis (SLK): superior bulbar injection + superior corneal SPK; associated with thyroid disease; treat with silver nitrate, vitamin A, surgical resection
Conjunctivochalasis: redundant loose bulbar conjunctiva; mechanical dry eye symptoms; treat with lubrication → surgical resection/cautery
Corneal Surface Disorders
Exposure keratopathy: incomplete lid closure (lagophthalmos) → desiccation → inferior SPK → erosion → ulcer; causes: CN VII palsy, proptosis, ectropion; treat with lubrication, taping, tarsorrhaphy
Neurotrophic Keratopathy
Decreased corneal sensation → poor epithelial healing + mucus accumulation
Causes: HSV/VZV, CN V lesions (acoustic neuroma, skull base surgery), diabetes, chemical burn, topical anaesthetic abuse
Stages (Mackie): 1 (SPK, mucus) → 2 (persistent epithelial defect) → 3 (stromal ulcer/melting)
Treatment: preservative-free lubrication, cenegermin (recombinant NGF, Oxervate), serum tears, scleral lens, tarsorrhaphy
Recurrent Corneal Erosion (RCE)
Defective epithelial-stromal adhesion (hemidesmosome failure); painful awakening (epithelium tears on eyelid opening)
Causes: prior corneal abrasion (fingernail, paper), map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy (EBMD)
Treatment: hypertonic NaCl (5%) drops/ointment, bandage contact lens, anterior stromal micropuncture, PTK (phototherapeutic keratectomy)
Dellen: focal corneal thinning adjacent to elevated limbal lesion (pterygium, filtering bleb) → disrupted tear film; treat underlying cause + lubrication
Limbal Stem Cell Deficiency (LSCD)
Loss of limbal epithelial stem cells → conjunctival epithelium invades cornea (conjunctivalization)
Signs: vascularized pannus, irregular epithelium, recurrent erosions, chronic inflammation
Causes: chemical burns (most common), Stevens-Johnson syndrome, aniridia, contact lens abuse, iatrogenic
Treatment: mild (lubrication, CL), moderate (allograft limbal stem cell transplant), severe (keratolimbal allograft + systemic immunosuppression)
Toxic keratopathy: preservatives (BAC most common) + topical anaesthetic abuse → mitotoxic SPK, non-healing ulcer; treat by stopping offending agent
Therapeutic Interventions for Ocular Surface Disorders
Conjunctival Interventions
Pterygium excision: bare sclera technique (high recurrence) vs conjunctival autograft (gold standard; <5% recurrence) ± mitomycin C
Limbal stem cell transplantation: autologous (unilateral LSCD, from fellow eye) vs allograft (bilateral LSCD; needs immunosuppression); cultivated limbal epithelial transplant (CLET) technique
Conjunctival flap (Gundersen flap): covers persistent corneal ulcer/perforation; provides vascularized support; rarely used now (superseded by amniotic membrane)
Mucous membrane graft: from oral mucosa; used in lid reconstruction, symblepharon, MMP
Corneal Interventions
Superficial keratectomy: removes anterior corneal lesions (Salzmann nodule, band keratopathy, scars); can be manual or PTK (excimer laser)
Recurrent corneal erosion surgery: anterior stromal micropuncture (for non-dystrophy cases) vs PTK (for EBMD-related RCE)

Amniotic membrane transplantation (AMT): biological scaffold; anti-inflammatory, promotes healing; sutured or sutureless (ProKera ring); for persistent epithelial defects, chemical burns, SJS acute phase
Tarsorrhaphy: partial or complete lid closure; protects cornea in lagophthalmos, neurotrophic keratopathy, persistent ulcer; temporary (reversible) vs permanent
Cyanoacrylate tissue glue: seals small corneal perforations (<2mm); temporary; covered with bandage CL
Congenital Anomalies of the Cornea and Sclera
Size & Shape Anomalies
Megalocornea: diameter >13mm; bilateral; no glaucoma; X-linked (CHRDL1); stable
Microcornea: diameter <10mm; may be isolated or with microphthalmos; associated with anterior segment dysgenesis
Cornea plana: flat cornea (K <43D); hyperopic; associated with sclerocornea; KERA gene
Keratoglobus: generalized corneal thinning + globular protrusion; associated with Ehlers-Danlos (type VI)
Anterior Segment Dysgenesis
Peters anomaly: central corneal leukoma + adherent iris/lens strands; Descemet absence centrally; PAX6/FOXC1 mutations; associated with glaucoma
Posterior embryotoxon: prominent Schwalbe line; seen in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome
Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: posterior embryotoxon + iris strands + iris hypoplasia ± glaucoma (50%); FOXC1/PITX2 mutations; systemic (dental, umbilical, facial anomalies)
Aniridia: PAX6 mutation; almost complete iris absence; associated with glaucoma, cataracts, nystagmus, foveal hypoplasia, Wilms tumor (WAGR)
Sclerocornea: cornea resembles sclera; opaque; unilateral or bilateral; associated with other anterior segment anomalies
Metabolic Disorders Causing Corneal Clouding
Mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS): diffuse stromal clouding; MPS I (Hurler/Scheie), IV, VI most common; enzyme replacement therapy
Cystinosis: cystine crystals in corneal stroma; photophobia; treat with topical cysteamine drops
Tyrosinaemia type II (Richner-Hanhart): corneal erosions + palmoplantar keratoderma + pseudoherpetic keratitis; FAH/HPD mutations
Degenerations and Depositions
Conjunctival Degenerations
Pinguecula: yellow-white fibrous/elastotic deposit on bulbar conjunctiva; nasal > temporal; UV-related; no treatment usually needed
Pterygium: fibrovascular growth from bulbar conjunctiva onto cornea; Stocker line (iron) at leading edge; nasal > temporal; UV + dust exposure; recurrence risk after excision
Conjunctival concretions: small white calcified deposits in palpebral conjunctiva; age-related; remove if symptomatic
Corneal Epithelial/Subepithelial Degenerations
Arcus senilis (gerontoxon): lipid deposition in peripheral stroma; normal >60 years; bilateral; if <40 and unilateral → r/o hypercholesterolaemia
Vogt limbal girdle: white chalky deposits at nasal/temporal limbus; type I (with clear interval) vs type II; age-related
Band keratopathy: calcium deposits in Bowman layer; interpalpebral; 'Swiss cheese' pattern (holes where nerves pass); causes: hypercalcaemia, chronic uveitis, JIAI; treat with EDTA chelation ± PTK
Salzmann nodular degeneration: grey-white elevated subepithelial nodules; post-inflammatory; remove with superficial keratectomy if symptomatic
Climatic droplet keratopathy (spheroidal degeneration): golden droplets in anterior stroma; UV/heat exposure
Mid & Deep Stromal Degenerations
Lipid keratopathy: white-yellow stromal lipid deposits; primary (rare) or secondary to neovascularisation; treat underlying cause, corneal transplant if severe
Amyloid deposits: rare; primary or secondary to inflammation
Endothelial Degenerations
Hassall-Henle bodies: peripheral guttae; age-related; normal finding
Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy: see dystrophies section
Drug-Induced Deposits
Cornea verticillata (vortex keratopathy): whorl-like epithelial deposits; amiodarone (most common), chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine, Fabry disease (α-galactosidase A deficiency)
Chrysiasis: gold deposition; deep stromal; after chrysotherapy (gold treatment for RA)
Silver (argyria): blue-grey conjunctival/corneal deposits; topical silver nitrate
Chlorpromazine: anterior capsular lens deposits + corneal/conjunctival pigmentation
Corneal Dystrophies
General Principles
Definition: inherited, bilateral, symmetric, not associated with systemic disease, not due to inflammation/infection
IC3D classification: Category 1 (well-defined gene mutation) → Category 4 (no clear genetic basis)
Treatment: depends on visual impact; options: epithelial debridement, PTK, lamellar or penetrating keratoplasty
Epithelial & Subepithelial Dystrophies
Epithelial basement membrane dystrophy (EBMD/Map-dot-fingerprint): most common corneal dystrophy; sporadic or AD (TGFBI); map-like, dot, and fingerprint patterns; causes RCE
Meesmann dystrophy: AD (KRT3/KRT12); fine intraepithelial vesicles; mild symptoms; usually no treatment needed
Lisch epithelial corneal dystrophy: X-linked; bands and whorls of microcysts; asymptomatic usually
Epithelial-Stromal TGFBI Dystrophies
Reis-Bücklers dystrophy: AD (TGFBI R124L); Bowman layer replacement by fibrous tissue; painful erosions; treatment: PTK, PKP
Thiel-Behnke dystrophy: AD (TGFBI R124H); honeycomb Bowman opacification; milder than RB
Lattice dystrophy
Type I (classic): AD (TGFBI R124C); refractile stromal lattice lines + amyloid; painful erosions; most common TGFBI dystrophy
Type II (Meretoja): AR (TGFBI); cranial nerve palsies + skin laxity (gelsolin mutation); systemic amyloidosis
Granular dystrophy type 1: AD (TGFBI R555W); discrete white 'breadcrumb' deposits; clear intervening stroma; treat with PTK or PKP
Granular dystrophy type 2 (Avellino): AD (TGFBI R124H); combined granular + lattice features; worse recurrence after keratoplasty
Stromal Dystrophies
Macular dystrophy: AR (CHST6); most severe; diffuse clouding from limbus to limbus; keratan sulfate metabolism defect; treat with PKP
Schnyder corneal dystrophy: AD (UBIAD1); central crystalline cholesterol deposits + arcus + central haze; hypercholesterolaemia in 50%; treat with PTK/PKP
Congenital stromal dystrophy: AD (DCN); diffuse flaky/feathery opacification from birth
Fleck dystrophy: AD (PIKFYVE); dandruff-like opacities throughout stroma; photophobia; usually non-progressive
Endothelial Dystrophies
Fuchs Endothelial Corneal Dystrophy (FECD)
Most common endothelial dystrophy; AD (SLC4A11, ZEB1, TCF4 triplet repeat); females > males
Guttae (excrescences on Descemet) → endothelial decompensation → stromal + epithelial oedema → bullous keratopathy
Symptoms: worse in morning (nocturnal oedema), glare, blur, pain (ruptured bullae)
Treatment: hypertonic saline drops → DSAEK/DMEK (gold standard for surgical treatment)
Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy (PPCD): AD (OVOL2, ZEB1, GRHL2); vesicles, bands, diffuse haze at Descemet level; usually asymptomatic; can cause glaucoma/iridocorneal adhesion
Congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy (CHED): AR (SLC4A11); bilateral diffuse ground-glass corneal oedema at birth; nystagmus; treat with early PKP
X-linked endothelial corneal dystrophy (XECD): rare; band-shaped clouding + 'moon crater' endothelium
Corneal Ectatic Disease
Keratoconus
Features
Progressive corneal thinning + anterior protrusion; non-inflammatory; bilateral (asymmetric)
Onset: puberty to 30s; stabilizes ~40s; associated with atopy (eye rubbing), Down syndrome, Leber congenital amaurosis, Marfan syndrome
Clinical Signs
Fleischer ring: iron deposits in basal epithelium at cone base (slit lamp, cobalt blue)
Vogt striae: fine vertical stress lines in deep stroma (disappear with pressure)
Munson sign: V-shaped indentation of lower lid on downgaze
Rizutti sign: conical reflection on nasal iris when light shone from temporal side
Apical scarring: late finding
Diagnosis
Topography: inferior steepening, asymmetric bowtie, I-S value >1.4D
Tomography (Pentacam): posterior elevation, pachymetric map (thinnest point), BAD-D score
Amsler-Krumeich grading: I (eccentric steepening, slight thinning) → IV (K >55D, central scar, pachymetry <400μm)
Treatment
Glasses/soft CLs: mild, regular astigmatism
RGP lenses: moderate; vaults irregular surface
Scleral lenses: vault entire cornea; severe + post-keratoplasty
Corneal crosslinking (CXL): halts progression; riboflavin + UV-A (Dresden protocol); standard vs accelerated; do before severe thinning
Intrastromal corneal ring segments (ICRS, Intacs): improve corneal regularity; contact lens tolerance; not curative
Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK): preferred if no hydrops; preserves host endothelium
PKP: if DALK not possible or failed; hydrops with scar
Acute hydrops: Descemet rupture → aqueous influx into stroma → sudden vision loss + pain; manage conservatively (hypertonic saline, CycloSporin); resolves with scarring
Pellucid Marginal Degeneration (PMD)
Inferior peripheral thinning (4-8 o'clock); cornea protrudes above thin band; 'crab-claw' or 'kissing birds' topography pattern
No Fleischer ring or Vogt striae; differentiate from inferior KC
Treatment: RGP/scleral lenses; DALK/PKP for advanced cases
Keratoglobus
Global thinning of entire cornea (unlike focal KC); often congenital; associated with Ehlers-Danlos type VI (lysyl hydroxylase deficiency)
Extremely fragile; risk of perforation with minimal trauma
Treatment: large-diameter keratoplasty; very high risk; protective eyewear essential
Systemic Disorders With Corneal Manifestations
Lysosomal Storage Diseases
Mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS): diffuse stromal clouding; MPS I (Hurler: severe; Scheie: mild), IV (Morquio), VI (Maroteaux-Lamy) most common; enzyme replacement therapy (laronidase for MPS I)
Fabry disease: X-linked (GLA gene); cornea verticillata; also posterior spokewheel cataract + conjunctival aneurysms; enzyme replacement with agalsidase
GM1 gangliosidosis: corneal clouding + cherry red spot
Metabolic/Amino Acid Disorders
Cystinosis: AR (CTNS); cystine crystals in cornea + conjunctiva + retina; photophobia; treat with topical cysteamine drops (dissolves crystals)
Wilson disease: AR (ATP7B); Kayser-Fleischer ring (copper in Descemet, peripheral, green-brown); sunflower cataract; treat with D-penicillamine or trientine
Gout: urate crystals in conjunctiva/cornea; band keratopathy
Hypercalcaemia: band keratopathy (calcium in Bowman layer)
Skeletal & Connective Tissue Disorders
Marfan syndrome: FBN1 (AD); lens subluxation (superotemporal); keratoconus; flat cornea; high myopia
Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: EDS type VI (lysyl hydroxylase deficiency) → keratoglobus; EDS type IV → spontaneous globe perforation risk
Osteogenesis imperfecta: blue sclera (thin sclera shows choroidal pigment); cornea may be thin
Nutritional
Vitamin A deficiency: Bitot spots (foamy white conjunctival plaques), xerosis, keratomalacia (corneal melting); night blindness; treat with vitamin A supplements (emergency if keratomalacia)
Endocrine
Diabetes mellitus: decreased corneal sensitivity (peripheral neuropathy), poor epithelial healing, reduced TBUT, recurrent erosions, increased infection risk
Hyperparathyroidism/hypercalcaemia: band keratopathy
Dermatologic Diseases
Ichthyosis: dry scaly skin; chronic cicatricial conjunctivitis, lid abnormalities, exposure keratopathy
Ectodermal dysplasia: absent/sparse lashes, dry eye, corneal scarring
Xeroderma pigmentosum: deficient UV repair (nucleotide excision repair); very high risk of conjunctival/corneal squamous cell carcinoma
Viral Infections
Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV)
HSV-1 (primarily ocular/orofacial); HSV-2 (genital; neonatal ocular disease)
Clinical Forms
Primary HSV: blepharoconjunctivitis ± follicular conjunctivitis; self-limited; vesicular lid lesions; treat with topical/oral acyclovir

Epithelial keratitis (dendritic ulcer): pathognomonic; branching dendrite with terminal bulbs; stains with fluorescein (ulcer) + rose bengal (halo); treat with topical antivirals (ganciclovir gel, trifluridine) ± oral acyclovir
Geographic ulcer: large amoeboid epithelial defect; often after steroid misuse; same treatment as dendritic

Stromal keratitis (necrotizing vs immune): necrotizing = viral invasion; immune = hypersensitivity to viral antigens; disc-shaped (disciform) = immune endotheliitis; treat: immune stromal → topical steroids + antiviral cover; necrotizing → oral antivirals
Endotheliitis: HSV-induced; keratic precipitates + Descemet folds + disc-shaped stromal oedema; treat with oral acyclovir + topical steroids
Neurotrophic keratopathy: post-infectious; reduced corneal sensation → poor healing; treat with lubricants, cenegermin, tarsorrhaphy
HEDS trial: prophylactic oral acyclovir 400mg BD reduces recurrence by 45%; important for stromal disease patients
DO NOT use topical steroids alone without antiviral cover in HSV keratitis
Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV)
Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO): V1 dermatomal reactivation; vesicular rash + pain
Hutchinson sign (tip of nose): nasociliary branch involvement → high risk of ocular disease (keratitis, uveitis, episcleritis)
Ocular findings: pseudodendritic keratitis (no terminal bulbs; heaped epithelium), stromal keratitis, disciform keratitis, anterior uveitis, CN palsies, ARN (immunocompromised)
Treatment: oral valaciclovir/acyclovir within 72h → reduces viral shedding, PHN, ocular complications; topical steroids for stromal/uveitis with antiviral cover
Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN): most feared; treat with gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclics
Adenovirus
Most common cause of viral conjunctivitis; epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC): serotypes 8, 19, 37; highly contagious
Pharyngoconjunctival fever (PCF): serotypes 3, 7; fever + pharyngitis + follicular conjunctivitis
EKC: follicular conjunctivitis + pseudomembranes + pre-auricular LN + corneal subepithelial infiltrates (SEI) at 7-14 days (immune reaction, not virus)
SEI: anterior stromal, cause glare/blur; treat with mild topical steroids if significant visual impact; may recur
No proven antiviral; supportive; highly contagious (contact precautions, no work/school for 14 days)
CMV Keratitis
Endotheliitis with coin-shaped lesions; KP; raised IOP; linear/dendritiform epithelial lesions
Immunocompetent or immunocompromised; recurrent anterior uveitis pattern
Diagnose: anterior chamber tap + CMV PCR; treat with topical ganciclovir + oral valganciclovir
Other Viruses
Molluscum contagiosum (poxvirus): umbilicated skin nodule on lid margin → follicular conjunctivitis + SPK from viral shedding; treat by excising/expressing the molluscum lesion
Papillomavirus (HPV): conjunctival squamous papilloma; sessile or pedunculated; treat with excision ± MMC/interferon; risk of malignant transformation (HPV 16/18)
COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2): mild follicular conjunctivitis; rarely corneal involvement; generally self-limited
Bacterial, Fungal & Parasitic Infections
Bacterial Conjunctivitis
Hyperacute (within 24h): Neisseria gonorrhoeae; copious purulent discharge; risk of corneal perforation; urgent systemic ceftriaxone + topical antibiotics + eye wash
Acute: Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, H. influenzae; mucopurulent discharge; treat with topical fluoroquinolone or broad-spectrum antibiotic
Neonatal (ophthalmia neonatorum): Gonococcal (<5 days) or Chlamydial (5-14 days); prophylaxis: erythromycin ointment at birth
Chronic: Chlamydia trachomatis (trachoma: serotypes A-C; follicles, scarring, trichiasis, blindness); STD conjunctivitis (D-K); treat with systemic azithromycin or doxycycline
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome: unilateral granulomatous conjunctivitis + ipsilateral preauricular LN; causes: cat-scratch disease (Bartonella), tularaemia, TB, syphilis; treat underlying cause
Bacterial Keratitis
Risk factors: contact lens wear (most common, especially soft extended-wear; Pseudomonas), trauma, ocular surface disease, topical steroids, immunosuppression
Organisms: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CL-related; rapid progression, ring infiltrate), Staphylococcus aureus/epidermidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae (hypopyon), Moraxella (immunocompromised, inferior)
Workup: corneal scraping + culture + Gram/Giemsa stain; confocal microscopy for fungal/Acanthamoeba
Treatment: intensive topical fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin/ciprofloxacin) ± fortified antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime); adjust based on culture; avoid steroids until pathogen identified
Complications: corneal scar, perforation, endophthalmitis; surgery if perforation (patch graft, PKP)
Fungal Keratitis
Risk factors: vegetable/plant trauma (filamentous fungi: Fusarium, Aspergillus), contact lens wear, tropical climate, immunosuppression (Candida in immunocompromised)
Filamentous: feathery infiltrate edges, satellite lesions, immune ring; Candida: more defined creamy ulcer
Diagnosis: corneal scraping, KOH prep (fungal hyphae), culture (Sabouraud agar), confocal microscopy
Treatment: natamycin 5% (first-line for filamentous), voriconazole (broad-spectrum, also intrastromal/intracameral injection), amphotericin B (Candida); long duration (6-12 weeks); avoid steroids
Acanthamoeba Keratitis
Protozoan; associated with contact lens wear in tap water/swimming; severe pain disproportionate to signs
Classic signs: ring infiltrate (late), radial keratoneuritis (early; pathognomonic), pseudodendrite, anterior uveitis
Diagnosis: confocal microscopy (double-walled cysts), corneal scraping + culture (non-nutrient agar with E. coli overlay), PCR
Treatment: PHMB (polyhexamethylene biguanide) + propamidine isethionate (Brolene); very long course (12+ months); antifungals not effective; PKP for severe/unresponsive
Interstitial Keratitis
Stromal vascularization + inflammation without primary epithelial ulceration
Syphilitic IK: most common cause historically; deep stromal vascularization; 'ghost vessels' (empty after healing); bilateral; treat with penicillin + topical steroids
Cogan syndrome: bilateral IK + vestibulo-auditory dysfunction (Menière-like); autoimmune; treat with systemic steroids
Scleritis
Microbial scleritis: Pseudomonas (post-surgery, especially pterygium excision with MMC), Mycobacterium, Herpes, fungi; necrotizing; treat with targeted antimicrobials + surgery
Immune-Mediated Disorders
Allergic Conjunctivitis
Seasonal (SAC) / Perennial (PAC): IgE-mediated; mast cell degranulation; itching = cardinal symptom; watery discharge; treat with antihistamines + mast cell stabilisers (olopatadine, ketotifen)
Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis (VKC)
Young males; tropical/subtropical; bilateral; seasonal (spring)
Cobblestone giant papillae on superior tarsal conjunctiva; limbal Horner-Trantas dots (eosinophilic aggregates); shield ulcer (superior corneal)
Ropy mucoid discharge; intense itching + photophobia
Treatment: topical mast cell stabilisers + antihistamines; topical steroids (short courses); cyclosporine A 0.05-1% drops; dupilumab for severe cases
Atopic Keratoconjunctivitis (AKC)
Older patients (20s-50s); associated with atopic dermatitis; perennial; bilateral
Papillae inferior tarsus > superior; corneal vascularisation + scarring; shield ulcer; risk of herpes simplex and cataract (PSC)
Treatment: as VKC + systemic immunosuppression (cyclosporine, dupilumab) for severe cases; long-term steroid complications (cataract, glaucoma)
Cicatricial Conjunctival Diseases
Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)
SJS (<10% BSA), SJS/TEN overlap (10-30%), TEN (>30%) based on epidermal detachment
Triggers: drugs (sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, NSAIDs, allopurinol), infections (Mycoplasma in children)
Acute ocular: pseudomembranes, symblepharon formation, severe dry eye, corneal epithelial defects
Chronic ocular: cicatricial entropion/trichiasis, LSCD, corneal scarring/vascularisation, severe dry eye
Acute treatment: amniotic membrane ASAP (within 48-72h) reduces chronic sequelae; aggressive lubrication; lysis of symblepharon; stop offending drug
Mucous Membrane Pemphigoid (MMP)
Autoimmune (IgG/IgA vs basement membrane proteins: BP180, BP230, laminin-332); subepithelial blistering + scarring
Stages (Foster grading): I (subepithelial fibrosis) → II (fornix foreshortening) → III (symblepharon) → IV (ankyloblepharon, dry eye, corneal keratinisation, blindness)
Diagnosis: conjunctival biopsy for immunofluorescence (IgG/IgA linear BMZ)
Treatment: systemic immunosuppression (dapsone 1st line, cyclophosphamide, IVIG, rituximab for severe); avoid surgery in active disease (triggers progression)

Ocular graft-vs-host disease (GVHD): after allogeneic bone marrow transplant; dry eye, conjunctival inflammation, cicatrisation; treat with intensive lubrication, cyclosporine A, scleral lenses, systemic immunosuppression
Peripheral Corneal Disorders
Marginal keratitis: peripheral sterile infiltrate from Staph hypersensitivity; clear zone between infiltrate and limbus; responds to topical steroids + antibiotic

Peripheral ulcerative keratitis (PUK): associated with systemic vasculitis (RA most common, GPA, PAN, SLE); crescent-shaped peripheral melt; workup for systemic vasculitis; treat underlying disease (systemic immunosuppression) + topical treatment; high mortality if untreated
Mooren ulcer: idiopathic PUK; painful; bilateral in 25%; undermined advancing edge; r/o systemic disease; treat with steroids, immunosuppression, conjunctival resection

Thygeson superficial punctate keratitis: bilateral coarse epithelial opacities; minimal conjunctival injection; unknown aetiology (possibly viral); treat with lubricants, mild steroids; recurrent but benign
Episcleritis & Scleritis
Episcleritis: superficial episcleral vessel inflammation; sectoral or diffuse; mildly uncomfortable; blanches with phenylephrine; usually idiopathic; treat with topical NSAIDs/steroids or oral NSAIDs
Scleritis
Deep inflammation; severe boring pain; does NOT blanch with phenylephrine
Anterior non-necrotizing (diffuse/nodular): most common; associated with RA, GPA, IBD, SLE in 50%
Anterior necrotizing: most severe; scleral melting; high associated mortality; requires urgent systemic immunosuppression
Posterior scleritis: pain, proptosis, choroidal folds, exudative RD; diagnose with B-scan (T-sign of posterior fluid)
Treatment: oral NSAIDs for mild; systemic steroids + immunosuppression for severe/necrotizing; treat underlying systemic disease
Neoplastic Disorders of the Conjunctiva and Cornea
Approach & Management Principles
Excisional biopsy: 'no-touch' technique for OSSN; 2mm clear margins; avoid seeding
Adjuvant topical chemotherapy: MMC 0.02-0.04%, 5-FU 1%, interferon α-2b; for OSSN, PAM with atypia, selected melanoma
Treatment options: observation, excision ± cryotherapy, topical chemo, radiation, enucleation/exenteration for advanced
Epithelial Tumors
Squamous papilloma: HPV-related (6, 11); fleshy fronds; limbal or fornix; treat with excision ± MMC/interferon; recurrence common
Ocular Surface Squamous Neoplasia (OSSN)
Spectrum: CIN (intraepithelial) → invasive SCC
Risk factors: UV, HPV (16/18), HIV, xeroderma pigmentosum, immunosuppression, old age, male
Clinical: gelatinous, leukoplakic, or papilliform lesion at limbus; interpalpebral; may have feeder vessels; +/- corneal involvement
Treatment: excision with 'no-touch' + cryotherapy + adjuvant MMC/5-FU; topical MMC or interferon as primary treatment in select cases; adjuvant treatment reduces recurrence
Sebaceous carcinoma: Meibomian or Zeis gland origin; masquerades as chronic blepharitis/chalazion; pagetoid spread to conjunctiva; high mortality; treat with wide excision + mapping biopsies
Pigmented Lesions
Conjunctival naevus: benign; cystic spaces (hallmark); immobile; treat with observation vs excision if concerning features
PAM (primary acquired melanosis): acquired flat brown pigmentation; no atypia = benign (observe); with atypia = precursor to melanoma (50% progress); treat with excision + cryotherapy ± MMC

Conjunctival melanoma: from PAM with atypia, de novo, or from naevus; nodular, multicoloured; high metastatic potential (sentinel lymph node biopsy); treat: excision + cryotherapy + adjuvant MMC/brachytherapy; systemic staging
Vascular & Lymphocytic Tumors
Pyogenic granuloma: vascular; rapidly growing red mass post-trauma/surgery; treats well with topical steroids or excision
Kaposi sarcoma: HHV-8; flat red lesion in fornix; AIDS-defining; treat with chemo, radiation, or excision
Lymphoma: usually MALT type (marginal zone); salmon-patch appearance in fornix; 25-30% systemic lymphoma; biopsy + staging; treat with radiation or systemic chemo
Toxic & Traumatic Injuries of the Anterior Segment
Chemical Injuries
Alkali Burns (more severe)
Liquefactive necrosis; penetrates rapidly (raises pH, saponifies cell membranes); damages all intraocular structures
Common: ammonia, NaOH (lye), Ca(OH)₂ (lime), KOH
Roper-Hall classification: I (limited epithelial damage) → IV (>50% limbal ischaemia + total corneal opacification = worst prognosis)
Acid Burns
Coagulative necrosis; protein precipitate limits penetration (self-limiting to some degree)
Exception: hydrofluoric acid penetrates like alkali (fluoride ions chelate Ca/Mg)
Management (EMERGENCY)
Immediate copious irrigation (>30 min; goal pH 7.0-7.4); do NOT wait to check pH first
Double eversion of lids + sweep of fornices; remove particulate matter
Topical steroids (first 7-10 days) + vitamin C (ascorbate, promotes collagen synthesis) + citrate (inhibits neutrophil infiltration) + cycloplegia + prophylactic antibiotics
Amniotic membrane transplantation: acute phase for severe burns
Long-term: treat limbal stem cell deficiency, scarring, symblepharon, dry eye, glaucoma (raised IOP common)
Radiation Injuries
UV keratitis (photokeratitis/arc eye/welder's flash): diffuse punctate epithelial erosions; 6-10h delayed onset; severe pain; self-limiting (24-72h); treat with analgesics + bandage CL
Ionising radiation: delayed corneal complications; dry eye, stem cell damage, cataract, retinopathy
Blunt Trauma
Corneal abrasion: epithelial defect; bandage CL + antibiotic + cycloplegia; avoid patching in CL wearers (Pseudomonas risk)

Traumatic hyphema: blood in AC; Berlin grading (I-IV by % AC filled); manage with activity restriction + topical steroids + cycloplegia; IOP monitoring; risk of rebleed day 3-5; sickle cell = avoid CAIs (sickling in AC)
Traumatic mydriasis: sphincter rupture; permanent; rule out CN III palsy
Iridodialysis: iris root avulsion; visible crescent at limbus; peaked pupil
Subconjunctival hemorrhage: benign; resolves 2-3 weeks; if spontaneous + bilateral → systemic workup (coagulopathy)
Penetrating/Perforating Trauma
Open globe: positive Seidel test (aqueous leak with fluorescein); DO NOT pressure on eye; shield (no patch); NPO; urgent surgical repair
IOFB (intraocular foreign body): CT orbits (not MRI if metallic); remove; risk of endophthalmitis + siderosis (iron)/chalcosis (copper)
Corneal foreign body: remove under slit lamp; rust ring removal (Alger brush); avoid corneal perforation
Traumatic endophthalmitis: post-penetrating injury; Bacillus cereus (soil/vegetable matter) + Staphylococcus; treat urgently with intravitreal antibiotics + vitrectomy
Corneal Transplantation
Eye Banking & Tissue
Donor criteria: exclude malignancy with anterior segment involvement, infectious corneal disease, prior refractive surgery, rabies, CJD, HTLV, HIV, hepatitis B/C (in most)
Storage: McCarey-Kaufman (M-K) medium (4°C, up to 4 days) or Optisol-GS (4°C, up to 14 days); organ culture (34°C, up to 35 days; European preference)
Minimum endothelial cell count for transplant: typically ≥2000-2500 cells/mm²
Penetrating Keratoplasty (PKP)
Full-thickness corneal replacement; trephine donor (0.25mm larger) and recipient
Indications: full-thickness scar, keratoconus (if DALK not feasible), corneal perforation, failed lamellar graft
Postop: high astigmatism (selectively cut sutures); risk of suture-related infection; topical steroids for months-years
Complications: allograft rejection (endothelial most serious; Khodadoust line of KP at graft edge; treat urgently with intensive steroids), primary graft failure, wound dehiscence
Deep Anterior Lamellar Keratoplasty (DALK)
Replaces anterior stroma + epithelium; host Descemet + endothelium retained
Indications: keratoconus, stromal dystrophies, anterior stromal scars; intact endothelium required
Advantages: no endothelial rejection; better long-term graft survival vs PKP; host endothelium intact
Big bubble technique (Anwar): pneumatic dissection of pre-Descemet plane (big bubble); facilitates deep dissection
Main complication: microperforation of Descemet (may need conversion to PKP)
Endothelial Keratoplasty
DSAEK (Descemet Stripping Automated Endothelial Keratoplasty)
Replaces host Descemet + endothelium with donor disc including thin stroma
Indications: Fuchs dystrophy, bullous keratopathy, failed PKP endothelial failure
Advantages over PKP: faster recovery, no sutures, no open sky, less astigmatism
Complications: graft dislocation (most common early; rebubble in AC), primary graft failure, rejection, interface haze
DMEK (Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty)
Thinnest possible graft: just Descemet + endothelium (no stroma)
Better visual outcomes vs DSAEK; lower rejection rate
Technically more demanding; higher dislocation rate; requires rebubbling more frequently
Cell injection / CIAES: emerging; inject donor endothelial cells + ROCK inhibitor; no graft surgery needed (future treatment)
Keratoprosthesis (KPro)
Boston KPro Type I: most common; PMMA optical core + titanium backplate; for eyes with multiple failed grafts or poor prognosis for conventional keratoplasty
Boston KPro Type II: for end-stage ocular surface disease (SJS, MMP); transcutaneous
Osteo-odonto-keratoprosthesis (OOKP): tooth root + lamina supports optical cylinder; for severe bilateral disease
Complications: infectious keratitis (lifelong prophylactic antibiotics), retroprosthetic membrane, glaucoma, vitreous wick
Rejection vs Failure
Rejection: immune-mediated; reversible with treatment; types: endothelial (most serious, Khodadoust line), stromal, subepithelial (nummular = like EKC), epithelial
Graft failure: irreversible endothelial decompensation; may need regraft
Rejection treatment: URGENT intensive topical steroids (every hour); systemic steroids if severe; maintain long-term topical steroids
Top 30 Exam Facts
Corneal endothelium does NOT regenerate; threshold for transplant ~400-500 cells/mm²
5 corneal layers: Epithelium → Bowman → Stroma (90%) → Descemet → Endothelium
Descemet is elastic and resistant to perforation; stroma is NOT
Limbal stem cells: source of corneal epithelial regeneration; located at palisades of Vogt
LSCD causes: chemical burns (most common), SJS, aniridia, contact lens abuse, iatrogenic
HSV dendrite: branching with terminal bulbs; stains with fluorescein (epithelial ulcer)
HEDS: oral acyclovir 400mg BD prophylaxis reduces HSV recurrence by 45%; no topical steroids alone in HSV
HZO + Hutchinson sign → high risk of ocular disease; treat with systemic antivirals within 72h
Adenovirus EKC: follicular conjunctivitis → subepithelial infiltrates at 7-14 days (immune); steroids for visual impact only; highly contagious
Acanthamoeba: CL + water contact; radial keratoneuritis (pathognomonic); diagnose with confocal microscopy; treat with PHMB + propamidine
Bacterial keratitis in CL wearer: Pseudomonas most common; treat with intensive topical fluoroquinolone
Fungal keratitis: plant/vegetable trauma → filamentous (Fusarium, Aspergillus); treat with natamycin ± voriconazole; avoid steroids
Keratoconus signs: Fleischer ring, Vogt striae, Munson sign, Rizzuti sign; treat progression with corneal CXL
CXL (corneal crosslinking): riboflavin + UV-A; halts KC progression; must have adequate pachymetry (>400μm)
DALK: preserves host endothelium; no endothelial rejection; preferred for KC over PKP
DMEK: better vision than DSAEK; lower rejection; technically harder; for Fuchs dystrophy and bullous keratopathy
Fuchs endothelial dystrophy: guttae → oedema; worse in morning; treat with DSAEK/DMEK
Graft rejection: Khodadoust line = endothelial rejection; treat immediately with intensive topical steroids
Alkali burns more dangerous than acid (liquefactive necrosis; deep penetration); FIRST treatment = immediate irrigation
Chemical burn treatment: copious irrigation first (>30 min); then steroids + vitamin C + citrate + AMT for severe burns
SJS/TEN: amniotic membrane transplant within 48-72h in acute phase reduces chronic sequelae
MMP: biopsy shows IgG/IgA at BMZ; systemic immunosuppression (dapsone, cyclophosphamide); avoid surgery in active disease
PUK (peripheral ulcerative keratitis): associated with RA/GPA/vasculitis; treat systemic disease urgently (high mortality if untreated)
VKC: giant papillae (superior tarsus) + Horner-Trantas dots + shield ulcer; young males; treat with mast cell stabilisers ± cyclosporine
Marginal keratitis: clear zone between infiltrate and limbus; Staph hypersensitivity; responds to mild topical steroid + antibiotic
OSSN: limbal gelatinous/leukoplakic lesion; UV + HPV risk; treat with 'no-touch' excision + MMC/interferon
PAM with atypia: 50% risk of conjunctival melanoma; treat with excision + cryotherapy ± topical MMC
Traumatic hyphema: avoid CAIs in sickle cell (risk of sickling in AC); monitor IOP; restrict activity
Open globe: shield, NPO, urgent surgery; positive Seidel test = aqueous leak; CT orbits for IOFB (not MRI if metallic)
Cornea verticillata (whorl deposits): amiodarone, chloroquine, Fabry disease (GLA mutation)
